coredata.dc:title: Iron-catalyzed synthesis of biowaste-derived magnetic carbonaceous materials for environmental remediation applications
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Bei
Jiang, Yiqun
Ding, Yuanzhao
Zhang, Jingjing
Balasubramanian, Rajasekhar
coredata.dc:description: The conversion of biowastes to carbonaceous materials (CNMs) has received increasing attention in the context of environmental sustainability. Herein, we report the synthesis of magnetic CNMs from the waste biomass, palm kernel shell (PKS), together with iron salts using a combination of hydrothermal and pyrolysis processes for the first time. Specifically, the addition of FeSO4·7H2O and FeCl3·6H2O to PKS tends to produce iron carbide and iron oxides, which contribute to the catalytic conversion of biowastes to various CNMs. The outcome of adsorption experiments reveals that the simultaneous adsorption of p-nitrophenol (PNP) and Cu(II) onto magnetic CNMs is mainly controlled by the availability of surface adsorption sites and the occurrence of intra-particle diffusion. The synergistic adsorption of PNP and Cu(II) is found when the initial concentration of PNP is less than 20 mg/L. With the concentration of PNP exceeding 20 mg/L, the adsorption of Cu(II) is suppressed due to preferential adsorption of PNP onto magnetic CNMs. The spent magnetic CNMs can be easily recovered, regenerated and reused in their adsorption capacity. Our study outcomes provide a novel strategy to synthesize biowaste-derived magnetic CNMs for practical environmental remediation applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the synthesis of MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600.
FE-SEM images of MPKS-H250-1-P600 (a-c) and MPKS-H250-2-P600 (d); TEM images of MPKS-H250-1-P600 (e-g) and MPKS-H250-2-P600 (h, i).

(a) XRD patterns of Fe2O3, Fe3O4, Fe3C, MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600; (b) Magnetization curves of Fe3O4, MPKS-H250-1, MPKS-H250-2, MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600; High-resolution XPS spectra of the deconvoluted C 1s spectra (c), O 1s spectra (d) and Fe 2p spectra (e) of MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600; (f) FT-IR spectra of PKS, MPKS-H250-1, MPKS-H250-2, MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600.

N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms (a) and pore size distribution (b) of MPKS-H250-1, MPKS-H250-2; (c) Time-dependent changes of the water contact angle of MPKS-H250-1, MPKS-H250-2, MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600.

(a) Comparison of the removal of PNP (c0 = 10 mg/L) and Cu(II) (c0 = 10 mg/L) by MPKS-H250-1, MPKS-H250-2, MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600; (b) Comparison of adsorption capacities of MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 for removal of PNP (c0 = 20 mg/L) and Cu(II) (c0 = 5 mg/L) in single and binary systems; (c) Effect of the initial concentration of PNP on the adsorption capacity of MPKS-H250-2-P600 in single and binary (c0 (Cu(II)) = 5 mg/L) systems; (d) Effect of initial concentration of Cu(II) on the adsorption capacity of MPKS-H250-2-P600 in single and binary (c0 (PNP) = 20 mg/L) systems. Commonly maintained experimental conditions: the dosage of samples was 0.5 g/L; the temperature was room temperature (25 °C); the initial pH was 5.0; the adsorption time was 24 h.

Adsorption isotherms of PNP (a) and Cu(II) (b) on MPKS-H250-2-P600 in single and binary systems. (c) Changes in adsorption capacities of MPKS-H250-2-P600 with respect to PNP and Cu(II) after washing it with 0.1 M HCl, ethanol, 0.1 M NaOH and heating it at 300 °C. (d) Recycling of MPKS-H250-2-P600 using 0.1 M NaOH for adsorption of PNP and Cu(II). Commonly maintained experimental conditions: the dosage of samples was 0.5 g/L; the temperature was room temperature (25 °C); the initial pH was 5.0; the adsorption time was 24 h.

Effect of humic acid and Ca(II) on the adsorption of PNP (a) and Cu(II) (b) by MPKS-H250-2-P600; (c) A schematic illustration of dynamic column adsorption; (d) Breakthrough curves of MPKS-H250-2-P600 for the adsorption of PNP and Cu(II) in binary systems. Experimental conditions: the concentration of PNP and humic acid was 20 mg/L; the concentration of Cu(II) and Ca(II) was 5 mg/L; the temperature was room temperature (25 °C).
The content of chemical bonds in MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 derived from the XPS spectra.
Pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic parameters for the adsorption of PNP and Cu(II) onto MPKS-H250-2-P600 in different binary systems.
Langmuir and Freundlich adsorption isotherm parameters for the adsorption of PNP and Cu(II) onto MPKS-H250-2-P600 in single and binary systems. (SE, standard error of qmax and kF, respectively).
Adsorption capacity of PNP and Cu(II) by reported biowaste-derived CNMs.

head.ce:title: Iron-catalyzed synthesis of biowaste-derived magnetic carbonaceous materials for environmental remediation applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, National University of Singapore, Singapore 117576, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, National University of Singapore, Singapore 117576, Singapore

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The conversion of biowastes to carbonaceous materials (CNMs) has received increasing attention in the context of environmental sustainability. Herein, we report the synthesis of magnetic CNMs from the waste biomass, palm kernel shell (PKS), together with iron salts using a combination of hydrothermal and pyrolysis processes for the first time. Specifically, the addition of FeSO4·7H2O and FeCl3·6H2O to PKS tends to produce iron carbide and iron oxides, which contribute to the catalytic conversion of biowastes to various CNMs. The outcome of adsorption experiments reveals that the simultaneous adsorption of p-nitrophenol (PNP) and Cu(II) onto magnetic CNMs is mainly controlled by the availability of surface adsorption sites and the occurrence of intra-particle diffusion. The synergistic adsorption of PNP and Cu(II) is found when the initial concentration of PNP is less than 20 mg/L. With the concentration of PNP exceeding 20 mg/L, the adsorption of Cu(II) is suppressed due to preferential adsorption of PNP onto magnetic CNMs. The spent magnetic CNMs can be easily recovered, regenerated and reused in their adsorption capacity. Our study outcomes provide a novel strategy to synthesize biowaste-derived magnetic CNMs for practical environmental remediation applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Biomass is the richest renewable resource in nature with promising environmental and energy-related applications [1]. The traditional management practices of waste biomass (biowastes) such as open burning has negative impacts on the environment and human health [1–6]. Due to a high carbon content (45–55% by weight) of common biowastes, reusing these carbon sources as carbonaceous materials (CNMs) provides a cost-effective and environmental-friendly approach toward environmental sustainability and circular economy [1,5,6]. Consequently, the conversion of biowastes into CNMs has been receiving increased attention [1–6]. Biochar is the most common biowaste-derived CNM, and is traditionally obtained from the thermochemical conversion of biowaste for environmental applications [3,4]. Additionally, hydrochar, activated carbon and porous carbon are also prepared with modified physicochemical properties, such as hydrophilicity, specific surface area and porosity [1,3,4]. In recent years, the conversion of biowastes to CNMs with carbon nanostructures such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [7], graphene [8,9], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [5,10] and carbon nanospheres [11,12] has been identified to have a great potential for a range of practical applications. Shi et al. used gumwood as a carbon source to synthesize CNTs via a microwave-induced pyrolysis process [13]. Sun et al. used FeCl3 and ZnCl2 as a catalyst and an activating agent, respectively, to prepare graphene-like nanosheets from coconut shell during the pyrolysis graphitization process [14]. Additionally, laser-induced graphene and flash Joule heating-induced graphene were obtained from various carbon wastes (e.g., coconut shell, biochar, discarded food and plastic wastes) [15].

However, the quality of biowaste-derived CNMs is highly variable because of the use of various types of biowastes, additives and conversion methods [16,17]. It is therefore necessary to develop an effective synthetic strategy involving an appropriate selection of biowaste sources, additive catalysts, and thermal treatment for suitable practical applications [1,5–12]. Specifically, cellulose [7,18–21] and lignin [17,22–24], the main components of lignocellulosic biowastes, are identified as efficient carbon precursors to produce CNTs and graphene, respectively, in which the cellulose-derived monosaccharides and aromatic lignin units serve as potential intermediates to form CNMs during thermal treatments. The addition of metal salts (e.g., Fe, Co and Ni) as catalysts significantly enhances the catalytic graphitization of amorphous biowastes to metal carbides, which in turn are used for the formation of graphitic carbon via dissolution-precipitation and formation-decomposition pathways [14,18,23–37]. For the dissolution-precipitation mechanism, the amorphous carbon from biomass is dissolved into the metal particles during the pyrolysis process, followed by precipitation as graphitic carbon, leading to the formation of CNMs [1,23–37]. For the formation-decomposition pathway, the amorphous carbon reacts with metal catalysts to form carbides, which are then decomposed into graphitic carbon and metal particles during the pyrolysis process [1,23–37]. However, the regulation of formation mechanisms remains challenging. Additionally, hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) [17,22], pyrolysis graphitization (PG) [14,21,23,24,29–31], microwave-induced pyrolysis [13,20], chemical vapor deposition (CVD) [32,34], and a combination of these techniques [38,39] have been developed as efficient thermal treatments to obtain biowaste-derived CNMs. However, the complex chemical components and structures of biowastes of diverse origins make it hard to synthesize CNMs in a controlled manner with quality assurance [1,6,33,34]. Furthermore, the mechanism involved in the formation of CNMs needs to be elucidated so that synthetic process parameters can be optimized for possible scale-up operations.

Herein, we present a novel strategy to synthesize biowaste-derived magnetic CNMs for environmental remediation applications. We selected the biowaste, palm kernel shell (PKS), as a carbon source because of the following reasons. PKS is a typical lignocellulosic biowaste generated in a palm oil industry with a rich content of lignin, and renewable. In comparison to traditional biowastes (e.g., glucose, starch and protein), the usage of lignocellulosic biomass is not competing with food production. We used FeSO4·7H2O (Fe(II)) and FeCl3·6H2O (Fe(III)) to catalyze the formation of magnetic CNMs. To achieve an efficient thermal conversion of PKS precursors to magnetic CNMs, we employed HTC as a pretreatment method, followed by the PG process. These CNMs were characterized using several advanced instrumental techniques. Accordingly, it was found that the addition of Fe(III) contributes to the generation of iron carbide (Fe3C), carbon nanorods and graphene-like sheets during the pyrolysis process by the formation-decomposition pathway. However, the simultaneous addition of Fe(III) and Fe(II) leads to the generation of Fe3O4 (ferrous ferric oxide (Fe(II) Fe(III) oxide)), which promotes the formation of graphene-like shell along with Fe3O4 nanoparticles via a dissolution-precipitation pathway. To explore potential environmental remediation applications of our novel materials, we investigated the effectiveness of using the PKS-derived magnetic CNMs as adsorbents for removal of p-nitrophenol (PNP) and Cu(II) as representative organic and inorganic pollutants. Both PNP and Cu(II) are commonly found in industrial wastewater and of environmental and health concerns [16]. Our experimental results reveal that the PKS-derived magnetic CNMs have high adsorption capacities for PNP and Cu(II) simultaneously, and can be separated, regenerated and reused successfully based on their magnetic properties.

Palm kernel shell (PKS) was separated from pristine palm kernel, obtained from Uni-Technologies Sdn.Bhd., Malaysia. The raw PKS was washed with deionized (DI) water, dried at 105 °C for 12 h, crushed into fine powder by an electrical crusher, and subsequently collected in the size range of less than 0.63 mm by a sieving process. The PKS powder obtained was then treated by an HTC reactor (Parr 4575A High Temperature/High Pressure Reactor, USA) at 250 °C. FeCl3·6H2O and FeSO4·7H2O (Sigma-Aldrich) were used as additives in the HTC treatment. During the HTC treatment, we added the two iron salts to obtain two types of magnetic PKS hydrochars, named as MPKS-H250-1 and MPKS-H250-2, respectively. To prepare MPKS-H250-1, 10 g of PKS and 5.078 g of FeCl3·6H2O were mixed with 100 mL of DI water and stirred in the reactor vessel for 10 min. 3 g of NaOH was dissolved into 50 mL of DI water. The NaOH solution was then added into the reactor vessel and stirred with PKS and FeCl3·6H2O for another 30 min. The mixture was then hydrothermally treated at 250 °C for 6 h using the HTC reactor. The black solid sediment obtained from the HTC treatment was separated and washed with DI water by the vacuum filtration with a 0.45 μm filter membrane. Eventually, the hydrochar, MPKS-H250-1, was obtained from the black solid sediment after drying at 80 °C for 12 h. MPKS-H250-2 was prepared by adding 3.502 g of FeCl3·6H2O and 1.801 g of FeSO4·7H2O in a manner similar to what was done during the synthesis of MPKS-H250-1. The content of Fe in MPKS-H250-1 and MPKS-H250-2 was adjusted to have the same amount (1.0 g of Fe). Additionally, reddish brown Fe2O3 and black Fe3O4 were also prepared in a similar manner as done with MPKS-H250-1 and MPKS-H250-2, respectively with no addition of PKS.

PG was employed at 600 °C for 2 h after the HTC treatment to obtain PKS-derived CNMs. In brief, 1.5 g of hydrochar (e.g., MPKS-H250-1 and MPKS-H250-2) collected from the HTC treatment was placed in the middle of a porcelain boat in a quartz tube maintained under N2 atmosphere (500 mL/min) within a horizontal tube furnace (TSH 12/50/300-2416, Elite Thermal Systems Ltd, UK). The products, denoted as MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600, were obtained from the pyrolysis of MPKS-H250-1 and MPKS-H250-2, respectively. Based on the yield of PKS and Fe during HTC and PG treatment, the mass ratio of carbon from PKS and iron oxides in the products is around 2:1. The addition ratio could be regulated by the application of materials [14,23,24]. The synthesis process was illustrated in Fig. 1 .

Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JEOL JSM-6701F, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-3011, Japan) were used to study the morphology of PKS, MPKS-H250-1, MPKS-H250-2, MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600. Energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) equipped on the FE-SEM system was used to investigate the elemental composition of the four samples. X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 ADVANCE, Germany) was performed to investigate the structural characteristics of Fe2O3, Fe3O4, MPKS-H250-1, MPKS-H250-2, MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600. The magnetization curves of Fe3O4, MPKS-H250-1, MPKS-H250-2, MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 were measured by a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM, LDJ 9500). The elemental characteristics of MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo VG Scientific, UK). The changes in the functional groups of PKS after HTC and PG processes were analyzed by the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Bruker Vertex 70 FTIR spectrometer, USA). The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 were analyzed by a gas sorption analyzer (Quantachrome Autosorb iQ C-XR, USA) at 77.35 K. The BET specific surface area of samples was obtained accordingly. The pore distribution curves were analyzed by density functional theory (DFT) algorithm. A contact angle analyzer (Ramé-hart Instrument Co, USA) was used to measure water contact angles of the samples by letting water droplets (2 µL) drip onto the slices, and resulting images were automatically captured at a given time. The zeta potential of the samples was determined using the Malvern Panalytical instrument (ZSU5800, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of PNP and Cu(II) in single systems
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption performance of PKS, MPKS-H250-1, MPKS-H250-2, MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 for removal of PNP and Cu(II) from the aqueous solution was compared in single systems by conducting batch experiments. For the adsorption of PNP (Sigma-Aldrich), the adsorbents were added into the PNP solution (10 mg/L). The initial pH of mixture was adjusted to 5 using 0.1 M HCl/NaOH. The adsorption experiments were carried out by shaking (150 rpm) at room temperature (25 °C) for 24 h. Similarly, the adsorbents were added into 10 mg/L Cu(II) solution for the adsorption experiments at an initial pH of 5 and 25 °C for 24 h. Cu(II) solution was prepared using CuSO4·5H2O (Sigma-Aldrich). The supernatants were separated with adsorbents by using a magnet beside the sample bottle and transferred to the vials for concentration analysis. The concentration of PNP was measured at 316 nm using an Infinite 200 PRO microplate reader (TECAN, Switzerland). Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS, Agilent 7700 Series, USA) was used to determine the concentration of Cu(II).

The dosage of PNP and Cu(II) added to the aqueous solution was varied based on which the dosage of MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 was optimized for effective removal of the two pollutants. Various dosages of the adsorbents (0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 and 2.0 g/L) were added into single systems of PNP (10 mg/L) and Cu(10 mg/L). The adsorption kinetics of MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 for PNP was also investigated by changing its initial concentrations (10, 20 and 50 mg/L). The concentration of PNP was measured at regular intervals (30 min) during the course of adsorption experiments.

According to the adsorption results, MPKS-H250-2-P600 possessed larger adsorption capacities for the removal of both PNP and Cu(II) compared to PKS-H250-1-P600. Hence, comprehensive adsorption experiments were carried out with MPKS-H250-2-P600. The adsorption isotherms of PNP and Cu(II) on MPKS-H250-2-P600 were studied with different initial concentrations in the range of 5–100 mg/L for PNP and 1–50 mg/L for Cu(II). Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models were employed to fit the data of adsorption isotherms based on the equations (1) and (2) given below. Additionally, the effect of pH on the adsorption of PNP and Cu(II) by MPKS-H250-2-P600 was investigated in the range of pH 3–9.

where qe is the adsorption amount of PNP and Cu(II) at equilibrium (mg/g); ce is the concentration of PNP and Cu(II) at equilibrium (mg/L); qmax is the adsorption capacity when the adsorbent is fully covered (mg/g); KL is the Langmuir adsorption constant (L/mg); KF is the Freundlich isotherm constant; and n is the heterogeneity factor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of PNP and Cu(II) in binary systems
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption performance of MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 for the simultaneous removal of PNP and Cu(II) was compared in a binary system. The two adsorbents (0.5 g/L) were added into the solution containing both PNP (20 mg/L) and Cu(II) (5 mg/L). The adsorption experiment was then performed in the same experimental conditions as with single systems.

MPKS-H250-2-P600 was selected to investigate the effect of changes in the initial concentration of PNP and Cu(II) on the adsorption performance of the following two binary systems. In the first binary system, 5 mg/L of Cu(II) was mixed with different concentrations of PNP (5, 10, 20, 40, 50 and 100 mg/L) for further adsorption experiments. The second binary system was carried by mixing 20 mg/L of PNP with different concentrations of Cu(II) (1, 2, 5, 8, 10 and 20 mg/L) for subsequent adsorption experiments. The adsorption conditions maintained during these experiments were the same as done with single systems. Additionally, Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models were employed to fit the experimental data of adsorption isotherms in these two types of binary systems.

Four types of regeneration methods were carried out to investigate the reusability of MPKS-H250-2-P600 in binary systems. After simultaneous adsorption of both PNP (20 mg/L) and Cu(II) (5 mg/L), MPKS-H250-2-P600 was separated with solution by using a magnet kept beside the sample bottle and subsequently washed by 0.1 M HCl, ethanol and 0.1 M NaOH, respectively. DI water was then used to remove the residual HCl, ethanol and NaOH on MPKS-H250-2-P600. Apart from the first three methods of washing MPKS-H250-2-P600 by 0.1 M HCl, ethanol and 0.1 M NaOH, the separated adsorbent was also heated at 300 °C in the tube furnace under N2 environment and this represents the fourth method for regeneration of magnetic CNMs. Eventually, the dry MPKS-H250-2-P600 was added in the same binary system for the next adsorption cycle. The adsorption conditions remained the same while using the regenerated adsorbent from the four regeneration methods. The regeneration experiment using 0.1 M NaOH was carried up to five cycles to test the reusability of MPKS-H250-2-P600 for adsorption of PNP and Cu(II) in the binary system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of interfering ions in single and binary systems
ce:section.ce:para: Humic acid (Sigma-Aldrich) and Ca(II) (CaCl2, Fluka) were selected as typical organic and metal interfering ions to investigate their effect on the adsorption of PNP and Cu(II). In the single system, four types of mixture were prepared: I- 20 mg/L of PNP and 20 mg/L humic acid; II- 20 mg/L of PNP and 5 mg/L Ca(II); III-5 mg/L Cu(II) and 20 mg/L humic acid; IV- 5 mg/L Cu(II) and 5 mg/L Ca(II). In the binary system, two other types of mixture were prepared: V- 20 mg/L of PNP, 5 mg/L Cu(II) and 20 mg/L humic acid; VI- 20 mg/L of PNP, 5 mg/L Cu(II) and 5 mg/L Ca(II). A blank experiment without addition of humic acid or Ca(II) was also performed by MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 in single and binary systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic column adsorption in binary systems
ce:section.ce:para: A dynamic column adsorption study was conducted in a fixed bed system with a continuous flow of the influent containing the targeted pollutants (PNP and Cu(II)). A mixture of PNP (20 mg/L) and Cu(II) (5 mg/L) was fed with a peristaltic pump (60 mL/h) to the bottom of adsorption column (length, 30 mm; inner diameter, 6 mm) packed with MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 (0.25 g), respectively. The inlet of the adsorption column was packed with glass wool to prevent the loss of adsorbents. All effluent samples were collected at given time intervals and analyzed.

The FE-SEM and TEM images, as shown in Fig. 2 and Fig. S1, exhibit the morphology of biowaste-derived CNMs. The SEM image of MPKS-H250-1-P600 (Fig. 2a) contains three types of nanomaterials, marked as type I, II and III. The enlarged SEM image of type I (Fig. 2b) shows stacked fusiform nanorods. Type II appears as wrinkled carbon sheets in the enlarged SEM image of MPKS-H250-1-P600 (Fig. 2c). Additionally, amorphous particles (Fe-containing phases) existing as type III are shown in Fig. 2a-2c besides the nanorods and carbon sheets. In contrast, only carbon sheets (as marked with dotted frame in Fig. 2d) and amorphous particles (Fe-containing phases) are observed in the SEM images of MPKS-H250-2-P600 (Fig. 2d). The different structures of MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 originate from the addition of different iron salts during the HTC treatment. As shown in Fig. S1a, untreated PKS shows relatively smooth surface with large chunks of biochar. After the HTC treatment with iron salts, MPKS-H250-1 (Fig. S1b) and MPKS-H250-2 (Fig. S1c) exhibit much rough surfaces. Notably, MPKS-H250-2 (Fig. S1c) contains clustered ion oxides particles, stacked in the layered carbon structure derived from PKS. The different Fe-containing phases produced from the HTC treatment lead to a different growth process of MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 during the subsequent PG process. The TEM images of MPKS-H250-1-P600 (Fig. 2e-g) confirm the three types of nanomaterials, including fusiform nanorods (type I), carbon nanosheets (type II) and amorphous Fe-containing phases (type III). Specifically, the enlarged TEM images (Fig. 2f, g) of MPKS-H250-1-P600 reveal the distribution of nanorods and Fe-containing phases, formed in the different stages during the PG process. A probable growth pathway of MPKS-H250-1-P600 is that amorphous carbon is transformed into graphitic carbon by a formation-decomposition pathway (Fig. 2f, g) and subsequently rolled into fusiform nanorods during the PG process [18,31,33,39]. In contrast, the graphitic carbon encapsulated iron oxides (as marked with dotted circle in Fig. 2h) are observed in MPKS-H250-2-P600. The enlarged TEM image of MPKS-H250-2-P600 (Fig. 2i) reveals that graphitic sheets grow around the iron oxide nanoparticle to form graphene-like shell, which is in line with the dissolution-precipitation process [18,24,30–35].

The elemental contents of MPKS-H250-1, MPKS-H250-2, MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 are obtained from EDS results, as shown in Fig. S1d-1g. Accordingly, the oxygen contents of MPKS-H250-1-P600 (22.6 wt%, Fig. S1f) and MPKS-H250-2-P600 (19.1 wt%, Fig. S1g) decrease after the PG process compared to those of MPKS-H250-1 (27.2 wt%, Fig. S1d) and MPKS-H250-2 (21.8 wt%, Fig. S1e), respectively. The Fe contents of MPKS-H250-2 (40.5%) and MPKS-H250-2-P600 (39.8%) are higher than those of MPKS-H250-1 (26.2%) and MPKS-H250-1-P600 (28.4%) due to the favorably formed Fe3O4 in MPKS-H250-2 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 during the HTC treatment, in which the stacked Fe3O4 particles are observed in the SEM images (Fig. S1c, Fig. 2d).

The phase composition and structural features of the samples (e.g., Fe2O3, Fe3O4, MPKS-H250-1, MPKS-H250-2, MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600) are examined by XRD, as shown in Fig. 3 a and Fig. S2. The magnetization profiles (Fig. 3b) of the Fe-containing samples were also measured at room temperature to identify magnetic components and estimate their magnetic separation performance. The hydrothermal treatment of FeCl3·6H2O in the alkaline solution produces the α-Fe2O3, which is confirmed with typical α-Fe2O3 planes of (012), (104), (110), (113), (024), (116), (214) and (300) (Fig. 3a and S2) [40,41]. The Fe3O4 particles (Fig. 3a and S2) prepared from the HTC process show typical characteristics of Fe3O4 lattice planes of (220), (311), (400), (511) and (440) (JCPDS, 19-0629) with 52.20 emu/g of saturation magnetization (Fig. 3b) [32,40,41]. The same diffraction peaks of Fe3O4 lattice planes are also observed in the XRD patterns of MPKS-H250-2 (Fig. S2) and MPKS-H250-2-P600 (Fig. 3a). The decreased saturation magnetization of MPKS-H250-2-P600 (11.25 emu/g, Fig. 3b) is ascribed to a possible thermal transformation of Fe3O4 while reacting with CNMs [32] during the pyrolysis of MPKS-H250-2 (16.67 emu/g). Additionally, the relative content of Fe in MPKS-H250-2-P600 (38.8 wt%) decreases in comparison to MPKS-H250-2 (42.5 wt%) (Fig. S1). However, a hybrid phase of α-Fe2O3 and γ-Fe2O3 in MPKS-H250-1 (Fig. S2) is identified by the diffraction peaks at 32.86°, 53.69° and 44.65°, which are assigned to (104) and (116) crystal planes of α-Fe2O3 (JCPDS, 33-664) and (311) crystal plane of γ-Fe2O3 (JCPDS, 39-1346), respectively [40–42].

The saturation magnetization of MPKS-H250-1 (7.48 emu/g) (Fig. 3b) is primarily derived from γ-Fe2O3 rather than α-Fe2O3 in MPKS-H250-1 since γ-Fe2O3 possesses much more ferromagnetic properties than α-Fe2O3 [40–43]. The prepared α-Fe2O3 has no magnetic response when a magnet is placed nearby the reddish brown α-Fe2O3. Notably, MPKS-H250-1-P600 exhibits a strong diffraction peak at 53.69° (Fig. 3a) and significantly increases saturation magnetization (30.13 emu/g, Fig. 3b) obtained from the pyrolysis of MPKS-H250-1 (7.48 emu/g). The diffraction peak at 53.69° is attributed to the formation of iron carbide (Fe3C, JCPDS 35-0772) in MPKS-H250-1-P600, which is also observed in the XRD patterns of reported Fe-containing carbon materials [30,32,33,44–46]. The existence of Fe3C enhances the saturation magnetization of MPKS-H250-1-P600, contributing to its high magnetic separation performance [44–46].

XPS was employed to investigate the difference in the chemical composition of MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 as shown in Fig. 3c-3e. Accordingly, the contents of chemical bonds in MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 are calculated from the XPS spectra and listed in Table 1
[17,22,24,39,40,42,47]. Specifically, MPKS-H250-1-P600 (CC, 81.2 wt%) and MPKS-H250-2-P600 (CC, 87.7 wt%) have high contents of CC (Fig. 3c) due to the deoxygenation and the formation of sp2-hybridized graphitic carbon during the PG process. The newly formed Fe-O-C bond is observed in MPKS-H250-1-P600 (25.3 wt%) and MPKS-H250-2-P600 (27.8 wt%), in which Fe-O-C is derived from the interactions of added iron salts and PKS during the HTC and PG processes [42]. Additionally, MPKS-H250-2-P600 possesses a higher content of Fe-O (57.4 wt%) than that of MPKS-H250-1-P600 (29.4 wt%), which is assigned to the favorable formation of Fe3O4 in MPKS-H250-2-P600 in comparison to the Fe3C in MPKS-H250-1-P600 [24,39,40,42]. Apart from the presence of ferric ion (Fe3+), much more ferrous ion (Fe2+) existing in Fe3O4 is found in MPKS-H250-2-P600 (27.8 wt%) than that of MPKS-H250-1-P600 (5.9 wt%). The FTIR spectra (Fig. 3f) are further used to analyze the change of functional groups in PKS, MPKS-H250-1, MPKS-H250-2, MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 [17,21,22,24,42]. Accordingly, the oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g., 3353 cm−1, OH; 1212 and 1025 cm−1, CO) derived from cellulose [21] and lignin [17,22,24] of PKS remain after the HTC treatment. Notably, Fe-O group (554 cm−1) is generated with the addition of Fe salts during the HTC treatment. After the PG treatment, the oxygen-containing functional groups of MPKS-H250-1 and MPKS-H250-2 are drastically reduced in MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600, which is in accordance with the results obtained from the EDS and XPS analyses.

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms (Fig. 4 a) of MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 show a hysteresis loop that is consistent with that of mesopores, which is assigned to the type-IV isotherm with an H3-type characteristic according to IUPAC nomenclature [14,30,47]. Accordingly, the pore size distribution (Fig. 4b) confirms the existence of micropores (1.69 nm and 1.76 nm) and mesopores (2.77 nm and 4.54 nm) in MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600. Notably, the presence of mesopores with a large size (15.31 nm) are identified in MPKS-H250-2-P600, which is ascribed to the slit-shaped pores from the layered carbon nanosheets [14,19,47]. In comparison to MPKS-H250-1-P600 (SBET = 294.02 m2/g), MPKS-H250-2-P600 possesses relatively larger BET specific surface area (SBET = 358.71 m2/g). The hierarchical porous structure and large surface area contribute to the mass transfer and adsorption of pollutants onto the sufficient number of adsorption sites [3,6,24,47].

The water wettability and hydrophilicity of MPKS-H250-1, MPKS-H250-2, MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 were characterized to estimate the potential applications (Fig. 4c). MPKS-H250-1 and MPKS-H250-2 show a high degree of water wettability and hydrophilicity with a rapidly decreased water contact angle within 20 s [47]. After the PG treatment, the water contact angles of MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 are above 118° after 20 s, exhibiting strong hydrophobic properties. The presence of hydrophilic and hydrophobic properties in biowaste-derived CNMs contributes to their adsorption of hydrophilic and hydrophobic pollutants in the environment [47]. However, a major challenge is to optimize the performance of multifunctional adsorbents with hydrophilic and hydrophobic surface characteristics for removal of hydrophilic and hydrophobic pollutants with a range of their concentrations. A notable outcome of our research is that a flexible strategy involving the synthesis and usage of adsorbents is provided to remove both hydrophilic and hydrophobic pollutants at the same time over a range of their concentrations. The addition ratio of biomass and iron salts could be altered depending on the ratio of hydrophilic and hydrophobic pollutants to be removed. If needed, we can also increase the dosage of adsorbents to increase the overall removal efficiency of the binary system. Additionally, the intermediate products of MPKS-H250-1 and MPKS-H250-2 show superior adsorption of Cu(II), which are suitable for decontamination of water containing a high load of hydrophilic pollutants.

Based on the characterization of biowaste-derived CNMs, the growth mechanism of MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 is proposed as shown in Fig. 1. During the HTC treatment of PKS, the main components of PKS, namely hemicellulose, cellulose and lignin, are converted to hydrochar via sequential hydrolysis, dehydration, decarboxylation, condensation, polymerization and aromatization processes [48]. Specifically, hemicellulose and cellulose are thermally unstable and produce various intermediates, such as monomers, oligomers, monosaccharides and furfural during the HTC treatment [18,20,32]. These intermediates further form secondary hydrochar after condensation, polymerization and aromatization processes [48]. However, lignin is much more stable than hemicellulose and cellulose due to the complex aromatic structure in lignin units [17,22,24]. The aromatic segments of lignin units constitute the primary hydrochar in the PKS-derived hydrochar [48]. Notably, the addition of different iron salts generates distinct Fe-containing phases anchored on the hydrochar to form MPKS-H250-1 and MPKS-H250-2. During the growth of MPKS-H250-1, a few hybrid α-Fe2O3 and γ-Fe2O3 (Fig. S2) particles are generated with hydrochar during the HTC treatment [40,41]. In contrast, abundant Fe3O4 particles are obtained from the co-precipitation reaction of Fe(III) and Fe(II) [40–42] and then stacked on the hydrochar to form MPKS-H250-2.

During the pyrolysis of MPKS-H250-1 and MPKS-H250-2, the hydrochar is catalyzed by Fe-containing phases to generate various CNMs in MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 (Fig. 1) [49–52]. For the growth of MPKS-H250-1-P600, considerable Fe3C (Fig. 3a) and graphitic carbon are generated via the formation-decomposition mechanism [1,25–31]. Specifically, amorphous carbon (e.g., amorphous char and volatiles) [51,52] derived from pyrolysis of hydrochar in MPKS-H250-1 reacts with Fe-containing phases (e.g., hybrid α-Fe2O3 and γ-Fe2O3 in MPKS-H250-1) to form Fe3C, which is subsequently decomposed into graphitic carbon and Fe-containing particles during the catalytic graphitization process [1,25–31,33]. The hierarchical structure of iron oxides, Fe3C and graphitic carbon is illustrated in Fig. 1 based on the TEM image of MPKS-H250-1-P600 (Fig. 2g). Additionally, numerous graphitic carbon segments derived from adjacent nucleation sites grow to form graphitic carbon nanosheets, namely graphene-like sheets [14,22,24,49]. In a critical condition, the graphene-like sheets can wrap into fusiform nanorods (Fig. 2e, 2f) to reduce the surface energy [49]. However, there are no nanorods except hierarchical structure of Fe3O4, Fe3C and graphitic carbon (Fig. 1) observed in MPKS-H250-2-P600 (Fig. 2h, 2i). Joy et al. [20], Shi et al. [33] and Wang et al. [49] reported that the biomass precursor, pyrolysis temperature and pressure determine the critical conditions for the growth of biomass-derived CNTs in a way similar to the chemical vapor deposition (CVD) process. However, consistent experimental conditions including the PKS precursor, pyrolysis program and atmospheric pressure remained during the synthesis of MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600. Thus, the different Fe-containing phases provide the critical conditions that regulate the growth of CNMs in MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600. Specifically, Fe3O4 particles existing as the main Fe-containing phase of MPKS-H250-2 are generated from the HTC process. During the pyrolysis process, MPKS-H250-2 prefers to form graphitic carbon by the dissolution-precipitation pathway, in which amorphous carbon of MPKS-H250-2 firstly evaporates onto Fe3O4 particles, dissolved by heating and then precipitates as graphitic carbon on the surface of Fe3O4 clusters (21–36 nm, Fig. 2h) [25]. The graphene-like shell (Fig. 3h) is further generated along the Fe3O4 particles. In contrast, less iron oxides particles are generated during the HTC treatment of MPKS-H250-1. Much more Fe3C is generated as the main Fe-containing phase in MPKS-H250-1-P600. The dispersive Fe3C clusters (6–11 nm, Fig. 2f) produce abundant graphitic carbon. The smaller size of Fe3C clusters in MPKS-H250-1-P600 contributes to the movement of the catalysts throughout the amorphous carbon matrix [18] and assembles different shapes of CNMs [50], such as nanorods and CNTs. However, the large Fe3O4 clusters and stacked Fe3O4 particles in MPKS-H250-2 limit the movement of catalysts to form different shapes of CNMs in MPKS-H250-2-P600. Thus, only graphene-like shell encapsulated Fe3O4 is generated in MPKS-H250-2-P600.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of PNP and Cu(II) in single systems
ce:section.ce:para: In the single systems, the pristine PKS shows an inferior removal of PNP (1.0%) and Cu(II) (5.6%) due to limited adsorption sites without HTC or PG treatment. For the adsorption of PNP (Fig. 5 a), MPKS-H250-1-P600 (94.8%) and MPKS-H250-2-P600 (95.0%) show significantly increased removal efficiency in comparison to PKS (1.0%), MPKS-H250-1 (1.7%) and MPKS-H250-2 (0.3%). The transformation of carbon skeleton, oxygen-containing functional groups and Fe-containing phases in MPKS-H250-1 and MPKS-H250-2 during the PG process regulates the adsorption properties of MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 for removing PNP [42,53–55] and Cu(II) [56–60] in single systems. In contrast, MPKS-H250-1 (97.8%) and MPKS-H250-2 (98.0%) possess higher removal efficiency of Cu(II) than PKS (5.6%), MPKS-H250-1-P600 (29.7%) and MPKS-H250-2-P600 (66.7%). The oxygen-containing functional groups in MPKS-H250-1 and MPKS-H250-2 possess strong electrostatic attractions toward Cu(II) cations [57,58]. Additionally, the iron oxides in MPKS-H250-1 (hybrid α-Fe2O3 and γ-Fe2O3) and MPKS-H250-2 (Fe3O4) also have strong affinity for the removal of Cu(II) [56,59]. Notably, MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 have relatively lower removal efficiency for Cu(II), which is attributed to the lack of strong electrostatic attractions between residual oxygen-containing functional groups and Cu (II) [56,59,60]. Thus, MPKS-H250-1 and MPKS-H250-2 show better performance toward the adsorption of Cu(II) in single systems.

With increasing dosage of adsorbents from 0.1 g/L to 0.5 g/L (Fig. S3a), the percentage removal of PNP significantly increases to more than 94%. There is no further significant increase of PNP when the dosage of adsorbents is higher than 0.5 g/L. In contrast, the percentage removal of Cu(II) continues to increase with the increased dosage of adsorbents from 0.1 g/L to 2.0 g/L. The relatively slow uptake of Cu(II) by the adsorbents compared to PNP appears to be related to the nature of adsorption mechanism involved in the two cases. Consequently, the optimized dosage of adsorbents is taken as 0.5 g/L in the single and binary systems to investigate the effect of other conditions for the removal of PNP and Cu(II).

The initial pH of the aqueous solution has an important influence on the adsorption capacities of materials [57,58,61,62]. The surface potential of materials and the dissociated form of pollutants in water change with the initial pH of aqueous media, leading to different levels of adsorbent-adsorbate interactions [42,53,61,62]. MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 (Fig. S3c) show a similar change of zeta potential with an increasing pH. The isoelectric points of MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 were determined as pH of 4.36 and 3.90, respectively. The point of zero charge (pzc) is the same as the isoelectric point if there is no specific adsorption of other ions than H+ and OH− [42,53,54]. MPKS-H250-2-P600, with higher removal efficiency of Cu(II) than MPKS-H250-1-P600, was selected to investigate the effect of initial pH (3–9) on the adsorptive removal of PNP (10 mg/L) (Fig. S3d) and Cu(II) (10 mg/L) (Fig. S3e). The adsorption of PNP is favorable with high adsorption capacities when the initial pH is in the region of 3 to 6 (Fig. S3d). When the initial pH is higher than 7, the adsorption of PNP by MPKS-H250-2-P600 is suppressed. The reason for this suppression is that the negatively charged surface in MPKS-H250-2-P600 (zeta potential = -35.21 mV at pH 7) induces enhanced electrostatic repulsion toward PNP (pKa = 7.08) because of its presence as dissociated phenolate ions at pH > pKa [42,53–55]. However, there is still a high adsorption capacity (15.08 mg/g) of PNP at pH 9, which is ascribed to the π-π interaction with MPKS-H250-2-P600 [53]. The adsorption of Cu(II) by MPKS-H250-2-P600 (Fig. S3e) increases with increasing pH from 3 to 5, and then decreases with increasing pH above 5. As the pH increases from 3 to 5, the zeta potential of MPKS-H250-2-P600 changes from +29.88 mV to −27.59 mV. Thus, the electrostatic attraction between the negatively charged MPKS-H250-2-P600 and Cu(II) cations is enhanced [57,58,61]. However, a new form of Cu(II), CuOH+, is formed at pH above 5, leading to a decreasing adsorption capacity due to the larger size of CuOH+ than Cu(II) [61].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of PNP and Cu(II) in binary systems
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption performance of MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 for a mixed PNP (20 mg/L) and Cu(II) (5 mg/L) was compared in binary systems as shown in Fig. 5b. The adsorption capacities of MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 for PNP and Cu(II) slightly increase in binary systems than those of in single systems, indicating a potential synergistic adsorption and negligible competition between PNP and Cu(II) in this binary system. In comparison to MPKS-H250-1-P600, MPKS-H250-2-P600 possesses comparable adsorption of PNP and higher adsorption of Cu(II) in binary systems. Thus, MPKS-H250-2-P600 was selected to analyze the competitive adsorption of PNP and Cu(II) in binary systems (Fig. 5c, 5d). Specifically, the initial concentration of Cu(II) is fixed to 5 mg/L, whereas the concentration of PNP increases from 5 to 100 mg/L (Fig. 5c). The adsorption capacities for PNP in binary systems are slightly higher than those of in single systems at initial concentration of PNP below 20 mg/L. The adsorption capacities for Cu(II) in binary systems also increase with an increasing concentration of PNP, indicating the synergistic adsorption of PNP and Cu(II) [61,63]. However, the adsorption capacities for PNP and Cu(II) decrease with an increasing concentration of PNP above 20 mg/L, which is ascribed to the competitive adsorption of PNP and Cu(II). In other binary systems, the initial concentration of PNP is fixed at 20 mg/L, and the concentration of Cu(II) is varied from 1 to 20 mg/L (Fig. 5d). The adsorption capacities for Cu(II) in binary systems are lower than those of in single systems, indicating the antagonistic effect of PNP on Cu(II) adsorption. However, the increasing concentration of Cu(II) has a negligible effect on the PNP adsorption as its adsorption capacity remains almost unchanged in binary systems (Fig. 5d). Thus, the adsorptive removal of PNP and Cu(II) in binary systems is sensitive to the initial concentration of PNP.

The kinetics of adsorption of PNP and Cu(II) onto MPKS-H250-2-P600 in binary systems is compared to that of single systems as shown in Fig. S3c (PNP, 20 mg/L; Cu(II), 5 mg/L), Fig. S3d (PNP, 20 mg/L; Cu(II), 10 mg/L) and Fig. S3e (PNP, 40 mg/L; Cu(II), 5 mg/L). The adsorption capacities for PNP and Cu(II) show a similar trend with the binary systems of Fig. S3c, 3d. However, the adsorption capacities for PNP decrease when the initial concentration of PNP increases to 40 mg/L in the binary system of Fig. S3e. The adsorption kinetics in binary systems is further analyzed by the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models, as shown in Table 2 . The pseudo-second-order model has higher values (>0.99) than those of the pseudo-first-order model for adsorption of PNP and Cu(II) in binary systems, indicating that multiple factors control the adsorption process [42]. Specifically, the type of surface adsorption (e.g., electrostatic interactions, ion exchange, π-π and hydrophobic interactions) matters in the initial adsorption that is far from equilibrium, and then the intra-particle diffusion dominates before approaching adsorption equilibrium [42]. Notably, the values of k2 for the adsorption of Cu(II) are higher than that of PNP, confirming the rapid adsorption of Cu(II) due to its smaller size than PNP [61,62]. Additionally, intra-particle diffusion of PNP is time-consuming when adsorption sites are gradually saturated, leading to a low value of k2 at a high concentration of PNP.

The adsorption isotherms of PNP and Cu(II) in single and binary systems are compared based on the Langmuir and Freundlich models as shown in Fig. 6 a, 6b and Table 3 . The Freundlich model gives a better fit with higher values of R2 and lower values of standard error (SE) for the adsorption of PNP than the Langmuir model, showing a multi-layer heterogeneous adsorption process [42]. The conjugate π regions and hydrophobic regions acting as adsorption sites in MPKS-H250-2-P600 are unevenly distributed with Fe-containing phases on the surface of MPKS-H250-2-P600, resulting in a heterogeneous adsorption process. In contrast, the uptake of Cu(II) has a similar value of R2 from the Langmuir and Freundlich models. It has been reported previously that the adsorption of Cu(II) tends to follow a monolayer adsorption process by electrostatic attractions and ion exchange mechanisms [61]. However, the adsorption sites from the residual oxygen-containing functional groups and Fe-containing phases (Fe3O4 in MPKS-H250-2-P600) are not well-distributed, which is thought to follow a heterogeneous adsorption with Cu(II) [61]. Notably, the value of R2 in single systems is higher than binary systems, which is ascribed to the effect of competitive or synergetic adsorption in the binary systems [61,63]. In comparison to the reported biowaste-derived CNMs (e.g. fly ash, samla coal, peanut hulls and humic acid) (Table 4 ), MPKS-H250-2-P600 shows competitive or superior adsorption properties for PNP [53] and Cu(II) [56] in single systems. Additionally, the attractive adsorption properties of MPKS-H250-2-P600 for organic and inorganic pollutants are confirmed in binary systems when comparing with those of wheat straw [61] and dried sugar beet pulp [63].

The reusability of MPKS-H250-2-P600 is investigated via different regeneration methods in the binary system as shown in Fig. 6c, 6d. Accordingly, MPKS-H250-2-P600 washed with 0.1 M NaOH shows the highest adsorption capacities for PNP and Cu(II) (Fig. 6c). NaOH solution serves as an eluent to desorb adsorbed PNP via enhancement of electrostatic repulsion between deprotonated MPKS-H250-2-P600 and electronegative PNP. Additionally, the adsorbed Cu(II) tends to form a precipitate of Cu(OH)2 at the alkaline condition, resulting in the desorption from MPKS-H250-2-P600. However, the adsorption capacities for PNP decrease after five recycling (Fig. 6d), which is assigned to changes in the surface or in the carbon skeleton during the regeneration operations [42,54]. Alternatively, PNP adsorbed on the MPKS-H250-2-P600 is not completely desorbed during regeneration, leading to a decrease in the number of active adsorption sites [42]. Notably, the adsorption capacities for Cu(II) remain almost unchanged even after five recycling (Fig. 6d). The efficient recovery of adsorbents by magnetic separation and regeneration enhance the recycling performance.

With MPKS-H250-2-P600 washed with 0.1 M HCl and ethanol, it is harder to achieve the desorption of PNP and Cu(II) for regeneration. Moreover, MPKS-H250-2-P600 heated at 300 °C exhibits comparable reusability for adsorption of PNP because of its loss through volatilization and thus desorption in the high temperature, leading to regeneration of the material. However, it is hard to desorb Cu(II) due to its strong retention onto the surface of MPKS-H250-2-P600 even at a high temperature, leading to a low recycling adsorption of Cu(II). Furthermore, the heat treatment of the adsorbent might change its surface properties and internal pore structure, leading to its undesired adsorption performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of interfering ions in single and binary systems
ce:section.ce:para: The addition of humic acid and Ca(II) slightly impedes the adsorption of PNP and Cu(II) onto MPKS-H250-1-P600 (Fig. S4a, S4b) and MPKS-H250-2-P600 (Fig. 7 a, 7b) in single and binary systems. In comparison to Ca(II), humic acid exhibits stronger interference in the adsorption of PNP and Cu(II). With the availability of efficient adsorption sites, the graphitic carbon skeleton in MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 possesses strong adsorption interaction with humic acid by π-π interaction [62]. Thus, the competitive adsorption of PNP and humic acid affects the uptake of the former. Notably, the adsorption capacities of MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 for PNP remain higher than 26.85 mg/g with the coexistence of 20 mg/L humic acid. In contrast, the competitive adsorption of Cu(II) and Ca(II) depends on the types of adsorption sites available for the uptake [64–66]. It has been reported that Cu(II) possesses stronger adsorption on adsorbents like goethite than those of Ca(II) [66]. Accordingly, the coexistence of Ca(II) slightly affects the adsorption of Cu(II) in single and binary systems. However, the synergistic adsorption of PNP and Cu(II) in the binary system is suppressed by humic acid and Ca(II) compared to the adsorption in the single system, possibly because of the large size of the former pollutant and the resultant pore blocking of the adsorbents. The interactions among adsorbents, PNP and Cu(II) weaken with the coexistence of humic acid and Ca(II) [56,62]. The outcomes of interfering ions indicate that the dosage of adsorbents to be added should consider the availability of surface active sites in them. More work is needed using surface complexation models to provide fundamental information for practical applications in natural water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic column adsorption in binary systems
ce:section.ce:para:
The most commonly used adsorbent in wastewater treatment plants is activated carbon [67,68]. The traditional wastewater treatment processes have low removal efficiency for emerging contaminants such as PNP [16]. Based on the outcome of our feasibility study, our adsorbents, MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600, have the potential to be used as fillers of adsorption bed for wastewater treatment in place of activated carbon. The dynamic adsorption performance of MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 was evaluated with a dynamic column system (Fig. 7c). The breakthrough curves of MPKS-H250-1-P600 (Fig. S4c) and MPKS-H250-2-P600 (Fig. 7d) for the adsorption of Cu(II) exhibit a similar removal process. The adsorption capacity for Cu(II) reached saturation after 12 h of dynamic adsorption. However, MPKS-H250-2-P600 retains its adsorption performance for PNP even after 12 h in comparison to that of MPKS-H250-1-P600. The dynamic adsorption capacities were calculated by the equation (3) [67,68]:

where qd is the dynamic adsorption capacity (mg/g); Q is the flow rate (0.06 L/h); t0 is the total adsorption time (12 h); c0 and ct are the initial concentration of PNP (20 mg/L) and Cu(II) (5 mg/L) in the influent and the final concentration in the effluent after the removal of PNP and Cu(II) due to adsorption; m is the weight of adsorbents (0.25 g).

MPKS-H250-2-P600 possesses higher dynamic adsorption capacities for PNP (29.12 mg/g) and Cu(II) (5.58 mg/g) in comparison to those of MPKS-H250-1-P600 (PNP, 23.76 mg/g; Cu(II), 4.86 mg/g). However, the dynamic adsorption capacities for PNP and Cu(II) are lower than the static adsorption in binary systems for MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600, possibly due to the less contact time (12 h in dynamic column adsorption and 24 h in static adsorption) and the dynamic mass transfer in the dynamic adsorption process seem to affect the adsorption capacities in comparison to the static adsorption system [67,68]. The effects of dynamic adsorption conditions (e.g., bed height, initial concentrations of pollutants to be removed and flow rate) need to be optimized for the practical applications.

The dynamic column adsorption of MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 has been performed, but possible practical applications as discussed below would be investigated in our future study. Franzreb [69] classified technological approaches to process magnetic adsorbents in scales ranging from a semi-pilot study to a large industrial scale. Notably, high gradient magnetic separator (HGMS) has been applied in the adsorption processes [69,70]. A comparison of packed bed adsorption and magnetic adsorption with a subsequent magnetic separation was conducted for removal of diluted pollutants [69]. Accordingly, magnetic adsorption exhibits significant advantages on the mass transfer and recycling efficiency [69]. The HGMS filter has been highlighted for the magnetic recovery of the spent magnetic adsorbents [70]. Additionally, magnetic adsorption has been applied in specific environmental scenarios such as oil spill remediation [71] and landfill leachate treatment [72].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption mechanisms of PNP and Cu(II)
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the characterization of biowaste-derived CNMs and the outcome of adsorption experiments, the surface adsorption sites in the adsorbents (MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600) contribute to the adsorptive removal of the substrates (PNP and Cu(II)). Specifically, the carbon skeleton (graphene-like structure and carbon nanorods from morphological characterization) of CNMs in MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 provides the main adsorption sites for removing PNP trough π-π and hydrophobic interactions [42,53–55]. Additionally, Fe-containing phases (Fe3C in MPKS-H250-1-P600 and Fe3O4 in MPKS-H250-2-P600 from structural characterization) are anchored onto the carbon skeleton by Fe-O-C bonds with the residual oxygen-containing functional groups. The Fe-containing phases with abundant Fe(110) and Fe3C(001) lattice structures provide active sites for the adsorption of Cu(II) [60]. Notably, the residual oxygen-containing functional groups (as revealed by the FT-IR and XPS analyses) have strong adsorption properties for removing Cu(II) by ion exchange [58] and electrostatic attraction [61].

Apart from the surface adsorption sites, the intra-particle diffusion also contributes to the adsorption mechanisms in binary systems. The competitive adsorption of PNP and Cu(II) in binary systems is significantly dependent on the initial concentration of PNP. When the initial concentration of PNP increases to above 40 mg/L, the adsorption of PNP and Cu(II) decreases because of the competitive adsorption. The large specific area of MPKS-H250-1-P600 (294.02 m2/g) and MPKS-H250-2-P600 (358.71 m2/g) provides abundant adsorption sites for the removal of PNP and Cu(II). However, the presence of micropores (1.69 nm and 1.76 nm) and mesopores (2.77 nm and 4.54 nm) contributes to the dominant porous structure of adsorbents. The high concentration of PNP preferentially occupies the limited adsorption sites from the micropores and mesopores. With a large molecular size, the adsorbed PNP impedes the intra-particle diffusion of Cu(II), reducing the uptake of Cu(II). In contrast, the increased initial concentration of Cu(II) has negligible effect on the adsorption of PNP due to the lack of interference from Cu(II) during the adsorptive removal of PNP.

In the binary system of PNP (20 mg/L) and Cu(II) (5 mg/L), the co-existence of PNP and Cu(II) slightly enhances the adsorption capacities of adsorbents for PNP, which is ascribed to the electrostatic interactions between the adsorbents and the pollutants. In the experimental conditions (pH = 5) maintained in our work, the negatively charged surface (e.g., zeta potential = -27.59 mV in MPKS-H250-2-P600) of the adsorbents is favorable to adsorb Cu(II) by electrostatic attraction. In contrast, the electrostatic repulsion between the negatively charged surface and partially dissociated PNP is unfavorable for the adsorption of PNP in the single system. However, when Cu(II) co-exists with PNP in the binary system, the adsorbed Cu(II) contributes to the reduced electrostatic repulsion, leading to improvement in the adsorption of PNP.

In summary, two types of biowaste-derived magnetic CNMs, MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600, were obtained by the HTC and PG processes with addition of iron salts. The individual addition of Fe(III) salt contributes to generate Fe3C for the formation of carbon nanorods and graphene-like sheets in MPKS-H250-1-P600 by a formation-decomposition pathway. In contrast, the addition of Fe(III) and Fe(II) salts tends to produce Fe3O4 predominantly, thereby promoting the catalytic graphitization of amorphous carbon to form graphene-like shell in MPKS-H250-2-P600 via a dissolution-precipitation pathway. Thus, the addition of Fe(III) and Fe(II) salts promotes the formation of various CNMs in a facile and controlled manner, exhibiting great potential in the production of high value-added carbon nanomaterials (e.g., CNTs and graphene) from biowastes. During the adsorption of PNP and Cu(II) in binary systems, the carbon nanomaterials in MPKS-H250-1-P600 and MPKS-H250-2-P600 serve as efficient adsorption sites for removing PNP by π-π and hydrophobic interactions. The presence of Fe3O4 in MPKS-H250-2-P600 leads to higher adsorption of Cu(II) than that of Fe3C in MPKS-H250-1-P600. The synergistic adsorption of PNP and Cu(II) is found in a low initial concentration (below 20 mg/L) of PNP. However, high initial concentrations (above 20 mg/L) of PNP in binary systems suppress the adsorption of PNP and Cu(II) in comparison to single systems. Additionally, MPKS-H250-2-P600 retains high adsorption capacities for PNP and Cu(II) even after five adsorption-desorption cycles while washing with 0.1 M NaOH. Our results provide a flexible strategy to the synthesis of biowaste-derived magnetic CNMs with excellent magnetic separation and adsorption performances. Further research is needed to explore other potential applications of biowaste-derived magnetic CNMs.
